input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin was developed according to a Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodology.  Structural modeling of the system’s components and interfaces was performed in SysML, while the component‑and‑connector architecture was expressed in ACME, with optional use of AADL to capture platform‑specific properties.  Behavioral aspects were modeled using MontiArc Automaton state‑machine descriptions for discrete logic and MathWorks Simulink for continuous‑time dynamics—these Simulink models were then converted to C++/Java executable code for simulation and integration.  The approach follows the SMArDT framework to integrate the multiple modeling notations and to manage the lifecycle of the hybrid artifacts, but no networking protocols such as MQTT or OPC UA and no version‑control tools were explicitly mentioned in the source documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestration follows the Digital Shadow Reference Model depicted in Fig. 1, expressed as a UML class diagram. The constellation comprises the core concepts of Purpose, DataTrace, Source, Asset, and Processing. Each Purpose defines a specific application intent of a digital shadow instance. DataTraces aggregate raw data records collected from Sources (e.g., sensors or control units) and link them to underlying Assets (the physical reference). Processing elements apply transformation, aggregation, and abstraction logic to produce context‑aware model updates. The system is structured into hierarchical layers where the Physical domain feeds raw data into the Digital domain, which is then exposed through standardized interfaces for downstream services. MBSE practices are employed to link the UML artefacts with SysML behavior descriptions and simulation models (e.g., Simulink) to enable end‑to‑end validation of the digital shadow.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models are structured across multiple abstraction layers (requirements, functional, logical, product/technical) following the MBSE approach. Functional and logical architectures are expressed with SysML (including IBDs and SysML v1.7 state machines) or with architecture description languages such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc. Behavioral dynamics are modeled through MontiArc Automaton or SysML state machines, while quantitative or simulation‑ready models are instantiated in MathWorks Simulink or implemented in general‑purpose languages such as C++ or Java. These heterogeneous model types are integrated via cross‑notation mappings and tool‑chain orchestration, forming the data‑rich, multi‑format backbone of the Digital Twin constellation.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The digital twin lifecycle follows an MBSE‑aligned sequence of abstraction layers: (1) requirements capture that establishes stakeholder needs; (2) functional architecture expressed through SysML diagrams and architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME or AADL; (3) logical architecture described with component‑connector models such as MontiArc signal flow or state machines; (4) product/technical layer realised via code generation to C++ or Java and simulation models in MathWorks Simulink. Transition management between layers is achieved through model transformations supported by the same tooling ecosystem, while governance of the entire lifecycle is guided by established MDSE methods including RFLP, OOSEM, SPES and SMArDT.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering of the Digital Twin employs a spaced methodology at several abstraction levels. Methodologies such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT guide the development. The structural model is expressed in architecture description languages (ADLs) like ACME, AADL, MontiArc, or SysML IBDs. Behavioural aspects are implemented using Simulink or other simulation tools, or general‑purpose programming in C++ or Java. Component‑to‑component interfaces and coupling are modelled to support versioning and incremental evolution. While explicit quality assurance or version control practices are not detailed, versioned MBSE artefacts typically track changes across the development phases.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly describes the system under study in Section III: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes'. It also details its components (motor, gearbox) and operational context (machining).

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the text mentions specific services provided by the digital shadow/twin. The abstract describes 'visualization in a dashboard' and 'live-data linking'. Section IV mentions the digital shadow enables 'integrated visualization within a unified dashboard'. These constitute visualization and monitoring services.

The extraction correctly identifies SysML and Simulink as tools used in the project (Sec. II.A, IV.A). However, it incorrectly claims the project uses ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SMArDT, which are listed only as general examples of methodologies and languages in the background section (Sec. II.A), not explicitly as the tools used for the MABI robot. Additionally, the extraction states 'no networking protocols such as MQTT... were explicitly mentioned', but Section IV.A explicitly states: 'We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT'. Due to these inaccuracies and the misattribution of background examples to the specific system, the score is reduced.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately and comprehensively details the MBSE abstraction layers, methodologies, and specific tools/languages mentioned in Section II.A. It covers the levels of abstraction (requirements, functional, logical, product/technical), specific methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT), and technical implementation details (ACME, AADL, MontiArc, SysML, Simulink, C++, Java) found in the source text.

The extraction effectively synthesizes information from Sections II.A and II.C regarding the purpose-driven methodology, modeling tools (SysML, Simulink, C++), and traceability. It correctly lists the guiding methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, etc.) and modeling languages. However, it infers that interfaces are modeled 'to support versioning and incremental evolution,' whereas the text primarily discusses traceability of data and models. Supported by quotes: 'sketched a method on how to create DSs' and 'facilitating traceability of how data within a DS has been generated'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the Abstract and Introduction explicitly describe insights generated (continuous identification of joint friction) and user interaction methods (visualization in a dashboard) to support decision making (improving motion accuracy). Therefore, the claim is incorrect. Supported by quotes: 'The resulting digital shadows enable the continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction' and 'integrated visualization within a unified dashboard'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the Abstract explicitly mentions implementing 'Asset Administration Shells (AAS)', which is a key specification/standard for Digital Twins. Additionally, Section II.A mentions the use of standard modeling languages like SysML and UML. Thus, relevant information on standards is present. Supported by quotes: 'implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS)' and 'ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML'.

block_6_judge.reasoning: The source document focuses on the technical methodology for creating a Digital Shadow for robot motion control, including data traces, simulation models, and Asset Administration Shells. There is no mention of data governance, ownership policies, privacy protection measures, compliance with regulations, consent management, or data sharing agreements.

The document describes the physical setup of the robot and mentions that 'The safety cell of the robot restricts the maximum working range' as a physical constraint. However, it does not discuss cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, threat mitigation strategies, fail-safe mechanisms, or general risk assessment approaches for the digital system. The characteristic description implies a broader scope of security and safety considerations which are absent.
